title: PATCH/DELETE /api/accounts return 404 for durable connections after redeploy — no rehydration on the write paths
evidence: web/app/api/accounts/route.ts:78, web/app/api/accounts/route.ts:101 (vs rehydration only at web/app/api/accounts/route.ts:59); web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts:214
reasoning: Verified against the cited code. Connection writes are durable: memoryStore.addConnection (line 206), setConnectionStatus (line 222), removeConnection (line 226) all fire-and-forget to osmo_connections, which persists across redeploy (SupabaseAccountsStore.upsertConnection/deleteConnection, store.ts:437-453). connectionById (memoryStore.ts:214) reads ONLY the in-memory Map — no durable fallback. GET (route.ts:59) calls ensureConnectionsLoaded(device.id) before reading, which rehydrates the Map from the durable store when empty (connectionsDurable.ts:10-15). PATCH (route.ts:78) and DELETE (route.ts:100-101) call store.connectionById(id) directly with NO ensureConnectionsLoaded call — I grepped the whole web tree and ensureConnectionsLoaded has exactly one caller (GET); there is no middleware that would warm connections. requireDevice/resolveDevice rehydrate only the device token (auth.ts:23-24), not its connections, so auth passes but the connection lookup still misses. On a cold process (fresh redeploy, or a load-balanced instance that never served this device's GET), connectionById returns null and both handlers return 404 'unknown connection' (route.ts:79-80, 102-103) for a connection that genuinely exists durably. This is a real read-your-writes regression on the write paths, not present on main (all-ephemeral). It self-heals only after a GET happens to warm the same process, but nothing guarantees GET precedes a PATCH/DELETE on that instance. Only manifests when the durable store is live (Supabase keyed). High is warranted: pause/resume/disconnect silently fail post-redeploy for a real user connection.
fix: In both PATCH and DELETE, add `await ensureConnectionsLoaded(device.id);` immediately after `const store = getStore();` (before `store.connectionById(id)`) — mirroring the GET path.

title: POST /api/sync/send fails with 409 'no live connection' after redeploy despite durable connection + durable OAuth token
evidence: web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts:33; web/lib/connections/degrade.ts:9; web/lib/enrich/person.ts:38; web/app/api/connect/rebackfill/route.ts:19 (only rehydration site: web/lib/connections/connectionsDurable.ts:10 called from accounts GET)
reasoning:
Verified against the cited code. The send handler (web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts:33) reads store.connections(device.id).find(...) and returns 409 "no live <platform> connection" at line 35-36 when find() is undefined. store.connections() (web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts:211-213) reads ONLY the in-memory Map with no durable fallback. Grep confirms ensureConnectionsLoaded is called in exactly ONE place: web/app/api/accounts/route.ts:59; there is no middleware.ts to warm connections globally. So on a cold process after a redeploy, if /api/sync/send is hit before /api/accounts, connections() returns [] and the send 409s.

The asymmetry the claim describes is real: OAuth tokens ARE rehydrated on cold read (web/lib/oauth/oauthStore.ts:13-17: getOAuthTokens reads memory, falls back to durable osmo_oauth_tokens via getAccounts().oauthTokens, then rehydrates), so freshOAuthToken succeeds — but the CONNECTION record lookup does not have this fallback. Connections are genuinely durable: addConnection (memoryStore.ts:206) and setConnectionStatus (memoryStore.ts:222) fire-and-forget to osmo_connections, and connectionsForDevice reads them back (accounts/store.ts:444-450). So the connection + its refreshable token are fully durable yet the send path can't see the connection. This directly defeats the branch's stated "connections survive redeploy" goal.

The same cold-read defect is confirmed in the other cited sites, none of which call ensureConnectionsLoaded: connect/rebackfill/route.ts:19 (409 "no <platform> connection"), media/route.ts:55 (falls back to placeholder() for a durably-connected Gmail account), enrich/person.ts:38 (drops LinkedIn enrichment to web-only), and degrade.ts:9 (markConnectionDegraded finds nothing to flag, so freshOAuthToken's refresh-failure path silently no-ops).

One scoping nuance on realism/severity: when Supabase isn't wired (accountsAreLive() false — keyless/demo and the test suite), the durable accounts store is itself in-memory, so a cold process loses connections everywhere and there's nothing to rehydrate; the bug is only observable in the live-Supabase production configuration. But that IS the configuration this hardening branch targets (the durable osmo_connections/osmo_oauth_tokens plumbing exists solely for it), so the defect is real and production-facing. Kept at high rather than critical because it's config-scoped to live Supabase and self-heals as soon as any /api/accounts call warms the process cache for that device.
fix: In web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts, add `await ensureConnectionsLoaded(device.id);` immediately before line 32-33 (the store.connections lookup), and mirror the same rehydrate call in connect/rebackfill/route.ts (before line 19), media/route.ts (before line 55), and enrich/person.ts (before line 38) — or, more robustly, fold the durable fallback into the store read itself so store.connections() rehydrates like getOAuthTokens does. Also ensure degrade.ts's caller path is warm so markConnectionDegraded can find the connection.

title: Free-tier quota cap is not race-safe: lost-update in durable bumpUsage + no re-check on consume lets concurrent requests exceed 15/week (and under-count)
reasoning: All cited code matches. quota.ts:60-67 peekQuotaDurable() only reads usageCount and returns allowed when used<15 with NO write/reservation. quota.ts:70-73 consumeQuotaDurable() calls accounts.bumpUsage() unconditionally with no re-check. store.ts:418-425 bumpUsage() is a non-atomic read-modify-write: next=(await usageCount())+1 then upsert{count:next} onConflict device_id. I verified the schema in web/db/migrations/0001_baseline.sql:46-50 — osmo_usage has PK device_id and a single 'count integer' column, no per-request row, no atomic increment, no unique guard. grep confirms zero RPC/increment/FOR-UPDATE/lock anywhere in web/lib/accounts or the migrations, so nothing mitigates the race. Two consequences both hold: (1) lost update — two concurrent bumpUsage calls both read N, both upsert N+1, so the counter advances by 1 while 2 credits were spent (under-count); (2) cap bypass/TOCTOU — because peek reserves nothing and consume re-checks nothing, N concurrent requests at used=14 all pass peek and are all served, exceeding FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK=15. route.ts:93-100 (peek→429) then :149 (consume after the Anthropic call) widens the window to the full model-call latency. The author's own comment (store.ts:419-420, 'An atomic SQL increment is the production hardening') acknowledges it's a known-unmitigated gap. Not overstated to critical: an aggregate spend breaker (route.ts:106-111) caps total Anthropic spend across devices and the per-device overrun is bounded by concurrency, so blast radius is limited — but the per-device server-side quota, described (quota.ts:1-3) as the real guard against a lying client burning the Anthropic bill, is defeated under trivial concurrency. High.
fix: Make consume atomic and reserving: replace bumpUsage's read-modify-write with a Postgres RPC that does an atomic conditional increment returning the new count (e.g. INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (device_id) DO UPDATE SET count = osmo_usage.count + 1 WHERE osmo_usage.week_start = excluded.week_start RETURNING count), reject when the returned count exceeds FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK, and reserve-before-call then refund on failure instead of peek-then-consume.

title: The entire Supabase durable code path is untested; every 'durability' test runs against the in-memory fallback
evidence: web/lib/accounts/store.ts:481-502 (selector always Memory in tests); web/tests/schema.test.ts:14 is the only tests/ reference to SupabaseAccountsStore
reasoning: Core mechanism confirmed by reading the code. resetAccountsForTests() (store.ts:499-502) unconditionally does `new MemoryAccountsStore(...)`, and getAccounts() only builds SupabaseAccountsStore when both SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY are set (store.ts:481-496). Grepping tests/: no test sets those env vars, mocks @supabase/supabase-js, calls createClient, instantiates SupabaseAccountsStore, or assigns g.__osmoAccounts. The only reference to SupabaseAccountsStore in tests/ is a comment at schema.test.ts:14. So the entire SupabaseAccountsStore class (store.ts:263-468) — mapUser/mapDevice/mapSub, onConflict targets, snake_case column literals, .maybeSingle()/.single() error branches, the linkDeviceToUser merge, purgeByDevice cascade — executes in zero tests. Durability tests (connectionsDurable.test.ts, hardening.test.ts, stripeWebhook.test.ts, quota) all assert on MemoryAccountsStore, a genuinely different class, so a wrong onConflict, broken mapper, or bad .single() error path would ship green. That part of the claim is real. I revised (not confirmed) and dropped severity from critical to high for two reasons found while reading. (1) schema.test.ts is a real static guard that partially mitigates one named failure mode: it parses db/migrations/*.sql and asserts every table+column the store relies on is declared, so a plain 'missing column in the migration' would go red. However this only checks a HAND-MAINTAINED CONTRACT list against the migrations DDL; it never reads the store's actual string literals, so a typo in a column name inside store.ts (that isn't also mirrored in the contract) is NOT caught, and it does not verify onConflict targets match a PK/unique, nor exercise any mapping/error-handling logic. So the guard narrows but does not close the gap. (2) The untested code is a coverage/quality gap, not a live production defect on its own — nothing here proves the Supabase path is currently broken, only that a regression in it wouldn't be caught. That is a serious testing hole for a durability-critical component but is high, not critical.
fix: Add a SupabaseAccountsStore contract test that runs the real class against an in-memory Supabase query-builder fake (a small object implementing .from/.select/.eq/.upsert/.insert/.update/.delete/.maybeSingle/.single over a Map keyed by table+PK), injected via g.__osmoAccounts, and re-run the existing durability assertions against it — so mapUser/mapSub, snake_case columns, and onConflict targets are exercised; alternatively gate CI on a Postgres/pgTAP integration run of the same store.

title: Stripe webhook idempotency test passes against an in-memory Set that doesn't dedup across restart/instances (and leaks memory)
evidence: web/tests/stripeWebhook.test.ts:73-80; web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts (module-level `const seen = new Set`, dedup via seen.has/seen.add)
reasoning: Read both cited files and the surrounding infra. web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts:18 declares `const seen = new Set<string>()` at module scope and dedups via seen.has/seen.add at lines 33-34 — nothing else. The comment at lines 16-17 explicitly admits this is "Per-process for now" and "moves to a durable processed-events table under 0-B." That durable table already exists: web/db/migrations/0002_durable.sql:103 creates `osmo_processed_events(event_id primary key, source, created_at)`, and web/tests/schema.test.ts:82 asserts its columns — but the route never references it (grep found no processed_events usage in the route, store.ts, or lib/license). So durable dedup is scaffolded but unwired. The test 'is idempotent on redelivered event ids' (lines 69-75) fires two POSTs in the same process and asserts the 2nd returns {dedup:true}; it is satisfied purely by the in-memory Set and would pass identically if durable dedup were never implemented — exactly the false-assurance the claim describes. Production impact is real on this branch, which is about surviving redeploys/multi-instance: (b) `seen` grows unbounded per process (memory leak) — unconditionally true. (a) cross-instance/cross-restart re-processing — real but narrower than a naive read: the DB handlers are state-setting not toggling (setActive writes a fixed active=true/false, route.ts:41-48), so a plain redelivery of the same event is harmless. The genuine harm needs Stripe's out-of-order redelivery across a fresh instance/restart (empty `seen`): e.g. a later customer.subscription.deleted handled first, then the older checkout.session.completed redelivered to a new instance flips a cancelled sub back to active. Stripe does redeliver and does not guarantee ordering, so this is a live correctness hole. Severity high is justified: it silently defeats the durable-idempotency guarantee this branch claims, on a billing/entitlement path, with a passing test masking it.
fix: Wire the route to the durable table: within the `if (event.id)` block, replace the in-memory Set with an atomic INSERT into osmo_processed_events (e.g. `insert ... on conflict (event_id) do nothing returning event_id`); if no row is returned, respond {dedup:true} — and update the test to assert dedup persists across a fresh module/instance, not just a second in-process call.

title: Durable connection row can be resurrected after DELETE by an in-flight fire-and-forget upsert (lost delete)
evidence: web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts:206, web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts:222, web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts:226; write bursts from web/lib/connections/backfill.ts:138
reasoning: Verified against the cited code on branch backend-hardening. In web/lib/connections/memoryStore.ts, addConnection (line 206) and setConnectionStatus (line 222) fire unawaited `void getAccounts().upsertConnection(c).catch(()=>{})`, and removeConnection (line 226) fires unawaited `void getAccounts().deleteConnection(id).catch(()=>{})`. The Supabase upsert uses { onConflict: "id" } (store.ts:437-442), so a late-arriving upsert re-INSERTs a row; deleteConnection is a hard `.delete().eq("id", id)` (store.ts:451-453). There is NO sequencing, version column, mutex, queue, or tombstone anywhere around these writes (grep confirms only the three raw fire-and-forget calls). Because each call is an independent, unordered HTTP request, a DELETE (via /api/accounts DELETE -> removeConnection) can execute at Postgres before a still-in-flight upsert from a concurrent backfill status transition (backfill.ts:148 "connected" / :155 "degraded", or the initial addConnection upsert). When the upsert lands last, it resurrects the deleted row in the durable store. Memory is correct (synchronous delete at line 225 wins in-process), but connectionsDurable.ts:10-14 (ensureConnectionsLoaded -> hydrateConnection), invoked from /api/accounts route.ts:59, reads the resurrected row back into memory after the next redeploy, so the disconnected connection reappears to the user and can resume syncing. The mechanism, evidence lines, and "no guard exists" claim all check out. Severity medium (not high): requires a specific concurrency window (disconnect racing an in-flight upsert, typically at backfill completion), affects only the durable store, becomes user-visible only after a redeploy, and involves no loss of user message content — but a user-removed account reappearing is a genuine correctness/privacy-adjacent regression.
fix: In removeConnection, await/order the delete after any pending upsert and guard resurrection at the DB layer — e.g. add an `updated_at`/version column and make upsertConnection a conditional write (skip if a newer delete tombstone exists), or serialize per-connection-id writes through a promise chain so deleteConnection always runs last; simplest robust fix: add a `deleted_at` tombstone column and have upsertConnection use `onConflict: id` with an `ignoreDuplicates`/WHERE-not-deleted predicate so a post-delete upsert cannot re-insert.

title: Durable quota bump is a non-atomic read-modify-write shared across instances — free-tier Anthropic quota is bypassable under concurrency
reasoning: Verified against the cited code. SupabaseAccountsStore.bumpUsage() (web/lib/accounts/store.ts:418-425) is a genuine non-atomic read-modify-write: it computes next = (await usageCount()) + 1 in JS, then upsert({count: next}, {onConflict: device_id}) which OVERWRITES count with the client-computed value rather than doing a server-side count = count + 1. The migration confirms osmo_usage is one row per device (device_id text primary key, week_start bigint, count integer — 0001_baseline.sql:46-50) and 0002_durable.sql:60-62 confirms the store-swap made this the authoritative durable quota counter, so the row is now shared across all Render instances (widening the race vs. the old in-memory counter). No RPC / atomic SQL increment / row lock exists anywhere in store.ts — grep found only plain upserts, and the code comment at store.ts:419-420 explicitly concedes an atomic increment is the missing production hardening. The peek/consume split in /api/suggest widens the window: peekQuotaDurable (quota.ts:60-67) only reads at route.ts:93, then consumeQuotaDurable→bumpUsage runs after the model call at route.ts:149; N concurrent free-device requests all read used < 15, all call Anthropic, then all write base+1, so the 15/week cap (FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK, quota.ts:9) is bypassable proportional to concurrency. The aggregate backstop does NOT cover this: spendBreaker.ts uses globalThis.__osmoSpend (line 11), self-described as in-memory/per-process (line 7), so on multi-instance Render it is per-process and only enforces aggregate day/month budgets (2000/40000), not a per-device cap. The claim is accurate on mechanism, scope, and the acknowledging comment. Severity is medium not higher because the counter still increments (under-counts rather than never counts), abuse requires burst concurrency per device per week, and the aggregate breaker still bounds catastrophic total spend — this is margin erosion, not unbounded spend.
fix: Make the increment atomic and race-safe: replace the JS read-then-upsert in bumpUsage() with a single server-side atomic operation, e.g. a Postgres RPC/UPSERT that does count = case when week_start = :ws then count + 1 else 1 end ... RETURNING count (ON CONFLICT (device_id) DO UPDATE with the conditional increment), so N concurrent callers serialize on the row and the 15/week cap holds across instances.

title: ?token= query-string device-token fallback now authenticates against the durable store on every requireDevice route — device tokens leak via URL/logs/Referer for full account access
evidence: web/lib/connections/auth.ts:27-39 (tokenFrom query fallback + async resolveDevice→durable), used by all routes e.g. web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts:18, web/app/api/account/delete/route.ts:11
reasoning: Verified against the cited code on branch backend-hardening. web/lib/connections/auth.ts:27-32 (tokenFrom) unconditionally falls back to the `?token=` query param when no Bearer header is present, and requireDevice() (line 35-39) calls tokenFrom() then resolveDevice(). resolveDevice() (line 18-24) now resolves the token against the durable osmo_devices table via getAccounts().deviceByToken() and rehydrates it into memory (line 23-24). I confirmed requireDevice is used by ~18 routes, not just SSE — grep found it in sync/pull, sync/send, account/delete (line 11), account/link, license/validate, license/reset, accounts, connect/link, connect/rebackfill, promo/redeem, checkout/session, etc. tokenFrom() has no route-awareness, so the query fallback applies to every one of these, including destructive ones (account/delete purges license+subscription+all data). The device token is a plain bearer credential minted by registerDevice()/upsertDevice() with no expiry, TTL, or rotation field (contrast the magic-link rows which carry expiresAt), so a captured token is replayable indefinitely. The git diff of commit 2fa80a5 shows the query fallback itself is pre-existing, but this branch is what makes a query-string token resolve durably and survive restarts (previously an in-memory-only miss after redeploy would 401), materially widening the window in which a leaked URL token stays valid. The leak vectors named (reverse-proxy/CDN access logs, browser history, cross-origin Referer) are standard and real for any URL-embedded credential. The one caveat lowering this from high: the Mac app sends the token via Authorization header, so `?token=` is a debug-only path not exercised by the normal client, making this primarily a latent exposure-surface / defense-in-depth weakness rather than an actively-triggered flow — consistent with claimed medium.
fix: In tokenFrom(), drop the `?token=` query-param fallback entirely (require Authorization: Bearer), or gate it to only the SSE GET /api/events route by passing the URL/method and allowing the query param solely there.

title: Sign in with Apple: verified email flag is discarded, so an unverified Apple email auto-links into an existing account
evidence: web/lib/auth/appleVerify.ts:62 (emailVerified computed) vs web/app/api/account/link/route.ts:28-31 (flag never read), web/lib/accounts/store.ts:317-323 (email-based auto-link)
reasoning: The claim is accurate to the code. verifyAppleIdentityToken computes emailVerified (web/lib/auth/appleVerify.ts:62) and exposes it on AppleIdentity (line 29), but a repo-wide grep shows emailVerified is never read anywhere. The sole caller, POST /api/account/link (web/app/api/account/link/route.ts:28-31), reads only id.sub and id.email and discards id.emailVerified, then passes the raw email to findOrCreateUserByApple (line 41). That function, when no user matches the Apple sub, looks up by email and, if a pre-existing user is found, updates that user's row to attach apple_user_id — i.e. it links the new Apple identity onto an existing email-based account with no verified-email gate (Supabase: store.ts:317-323; memory: store.ts:156-160). Existing email accounts are created via the magic-link path (/api/auth/verify -> findOrCreateUserByEmail), so a merge grants the Apple identity full access to that account plus its subscription. Sign in with Apple can return email_verified=false for an unconfirmed non-Apple email on an Apple ID; trusting such an email to auto-link is exactly the account-linking/pre-hijack weakness the verified flag exists to prevent. The file's own header comment (route.ts:3) advertises it as the account-takeover fix ("trust only the token"), making the dropped verified bit a clear oversight, not an intended design. Exploitability is bounded by Apple's own controls over which emails appear in tokens (attacker must get Apple to emit the victim's email in a token), and it only matters when the victim already has an email account — hence medium, not high. The secondary nonce point (appleVerify.ts:57 accepts both sha256(nonce) and the raw nonce) is also accurate but is minor defense-in-depth and does not change the verdict.
fix: In web/app/api/account/link/route.ts, after verifying the token, drop the email when unverified before linking: set email = id.emailVerified ? id.email : null (so an unverified Apple email cannot auto-merge into an existing account and instead forces the email-required-to-create path).

title: Spend circuit-breaker under-counts real Anthropic calls: recordModelCall() fires once but callAnthropic() makes up to 3 billable requests
reasoning: Confirmed against the code. recordModelCall() is invoked exactly once per POST at route.ts:112, before callAnthropic() runs. callAnthropic() (route.ts:161-186) loops attempt=0..2 and issues a real fetch POST to https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages on every attempt, retrying on RETRYABLE_STATUS {429,500,502,503,529} (route.ts:157) and on network errors (route.ts:179-183), up to 3 attempts. So one user request can generate up to 3 real upstream POSTs while the day/month counters increment by only 1. The spend breaker (spendBreaker.ts:34-35) trips on dayCount>=dailyMax / monthCount>=monthlyMax, so those counters measure requests-that-passed-the-breaker, not real model calls — contradicting the function's own contract ("Count one real model call", spendBreaker.ts:39) and the header comment that bills it as the aggregate backstop covering Pro and auth bypass. An existing test even proves the mechanism: "retries a transient 529 then succeeds" (hardening.test.ts:90-102) asserts n===3 fetches for a single request. So the claim's mechanism and evidence are accurate and it is NOT already handled — there is no per-attempt counting anywhere (recordModelCall has exactly one call site). I revise severity from high to medium: the under-count is bounded at 3x (max attempts), only occurs during transient/overload conditions rather than normal 1st-attempt-success operation, and retried 429/529 responses are rejected (not completed generations) so they are largely unbilled — the truly duplicated billable spend is the smaller 5xx/network-then-200 path. It is a genuine correctness gap in a cost-protection control, but the blast radius is capped and incident-gated, not unbounded or routine.
fix: Count per real attempt: move recordModelCall() out of route.ts:112 and invoke it inside callAnthropic()'s loop immediately before each fetch (route.ts:170) so every billable upstream POST increments the day/month counters; keep breakerTripped() checked at route.ts:106 to short-circuit before the first attempt.

title: setConnectionStatus persists status transitions but never backfillProgress → durable row stuck at progress 0 mid-backfill
reasoning: Verified against the cited code and all downstream sites. memoryStore.ts:218-222: setConnectionStatus computes statusChanged = c.status !== status and only calls upsertConnection (durable) when statusChanged is true. backfill.ts:137-138 calls setConnectionStatus(accountId,"backfilling",progress) each page while status stays "backfilling", so statusChanged is false and the climbing progress is never persisted — memory updates c.backfillProgress but durable stays at whatever was last written. Connections are created with backfillProgress:0 (connect/notify:37, oauth callbacks, accounts route:41, mock.ts:44), and addConnection (memoryStore.ts:204-207) persists that 0; there is no status transition during backfill that would trigger a durable write (it's already "backfilling"). So the only durable progress value mid-backfill is 0. On redeploy, ensureConnectionsLoaded (connectionsDurable.ts:10-15) rehydrates via hydrateConnection (no re-persist) from the durable row, giving status="backfilling", backfillProgress=0. GET /api/accounts (route.ts:59-64) returns that connection's fields verbatim (only deviceId stripped), so it reports 0% for a backfill that was e.g. 80% done. And backfill is fire-and-forget: backfillConnection is only void-launched from notify/rebackfill/accounts-POST and the orphan-adopt path, which is disabled by default (OSMO_ADOPT_ORPHANS !== "1" returns early). ensureConnectionsLoaded does not relaunch it, so nothing resumes or completes the stuck backfill in the default config — the 0% is permanent for that row. All six sub-claims hold. Severity stays low: the impact is a cosmetically wrong progress percentage on a narrow redeploy-mid-backfill window in keyless/durable mode; the comment at line 221 shows the persist-on-transition-only behavior is intentional to avoid write amplification. (The deeper problem — that the whole backfill is silently dropped on redeploy — is a separate fire-and-forget gap, not this progress-persistence claim.)
fix: In setConnectionStatus, also persist when progress meaningfully changed while status is unchanged, e.g. change the guard to: if (statusChanged || (progress !== undefined && progress - prevProgress >= 0.1)) void getAccounts().upsertConnection(c).catch(()=>{}); (throttled so it still avoids per-tick write amplification), so the durable row tracks backfill progress and rehydrates non-zero.

title: Migration 0001 osmo_magic_links is missing the created_at column that exists in the live table
evidence: web/db/migrations/0001_baseline.sql:52-57 (no created_at) vs live osmo_magic_links.created_at (timestamptz NOT NULL default now())
reasoning: All anchors verified. (1) Read web/db/migrations/0001_baseline.sql:52-57: osmo_magic_links is defined with only token/email/expires_at/used — no created_at. The file header (lines 1-4) explicitly asserts these tables "mirror EXACTLY" live and the file "is now the source of truth," so this is a real breach of the file's own stated contract. (2) Confirmed live divergence two ways against project nxibeiykcgxpbmkeadth: Supabase list_tables and a direct information_schema.columns query both show public.osmo_magic_links has a 5th column created_at = timestamp with time zone, is_nullable NO, default now() — exactly as claimed. (3) Correctly scoped as non-runtime: SupabaseAccountsStore.createMagicLink (store.ts:388) inserts only {token,email,expires_at,used}; consumeMagicLink (392-395) does select("*") but reads only used/expires_at/email; the MagicLinkRow interface (store.ts:48) has no createdAt field. Since created_at has a default now() in live, existing inserts succeed. Not already handled: the migration uses `create table if not exists`, so re-running 0001 against live would NOT backfill the missing column (idempotent no-op on an existing table), and no later migration adds it — the drift is not self-healing. Impact is confined to documentation/drift: a schema rebuilt from 0001 in a fresh environment would omit a column production has, and 0001 cannot be trusted as an exact snapshot. That is exactly a low-severity finding; claimed severity low is appropriate.
fix: In web/db/migrations/0001_baseline.sql, add `created_at timestamptz not null default now()` to the osmo_magic_links table (after the `used` column at line 56) so the baseline matches live.

title: Stripe webhook idempotency and replay defense are in-memory/per-process — replay possible after restart or on a second instance
reasoning: All cited facts verified in the code. (1) Signature verification is sound: constant-time hex compare with length guard and 300s timestamp tolerance (web/lib/license/stripeSig.ts:28,32-34). (2) The webhook route's only replay/idempotency defense is a module-level in-memory `const seen = new Set<string>()` (web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts:18), checked/populated at lines 32-35. It lives in per-process module scope, so it is empty after any redeploy/restart and is independent per Render instance. (3) The state it protects IS durable: setActive() calls getAccounts().setSubscriptionForDevice/User, and the live store is SupabaseAccountsStore which upserts into osmo_subscriptions (store.ts:344-358, 481-490) — the same source /api/license/validate reads. So a replayed event re-writes persistent, cross-instance subscription state. (4) The durable osmo_processed_events table (0002_durable.sql:103-107) is genuinely unused by any route — grep finds it only in the migration and a schema test (web/tests/schema.test.ts:82), never imported/queried in app code. The route's own comment (lines 16-17) admits this: "Per-process for now; moves to a durable processed-events table under 0-B." Impact is bounded and low: replay is only possible within the 300s signature-tolerance window (stale timestamps are rejected), and the handlers set ABSOLUTE state (active=true / active=false), so replaying an event usually re-applies the state it already produced. Real harm (re-flipping) requires an out-of-order replay of a captured event whose timestamp is still within 300s while a different event has since changed state — a narrow race. The unbounded Set is a minor slow-leak. Net: real but low severity, matching the claim.
fix: Replace the in-memory `seen` Set with a durable dedup: before processing, INSERT event.id into osmo_processed_events (source='stripe') and treat a primary-key conflict as already-processed (e.g. `insert ... on conflict do nothing` and if 0 rows affected, return {ok:true,dedup:true}); keep the current in-memory Set only as the keyless/dev fallback.

title: A credit is consumed on a successful-but-empty (or all-non-text) Anthropic 200 response
reasoning: Verified against web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:139-154. After a 200 from Anthropic, text is built at 140-144 by filtering content blocks to type==='text', mapping b.text, joining and .trim(). If the 200 carries no text (empty content array, or a content-filtering/refusal stop with stop_reason but no text block), text becomes ''. The code then unconditionally fires metric('draft.ok') (145) and, for free non-unlimited devices, calls consumeQuotaDurable (149), which I confirmed in lib/license/quota.ts:70 unconditionally decrements the durable counter. It returns {text:''} with x-osmo-drafts-remaining decremented (152-154). There is no `if (!text)` guard anywhere before the consume. This directly contradicts the file's own line-147 promise ('Consume the free-tier credit ONLY now that we have a real draft (consume-on-success)') — an empty string is not a real, usable draft, yet a free user is charged. So the mechanism as described is real and not already handled. Two caveats that lower the severity from the claimed medium to low: (1) The specific tool_use/thinking-block scenario in the claim is not actually reachable with this request — anthropicBody (114-122) defines no `tools` and enables no `thinking`, so those block types will not appear. (2) The genuinely reachable triggers (empty content array, or a content-filtering/refusal 200) are rare edge cases, and the harm is bounded to one wrongly-burned free draft (1 of FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK), with no security/spend/data impact — the Anthropic call was already paid for regardless. The client-facing effect is a blank result the strip cannot render plus a silently decremented quota. Real but low-frequency and low-impact.
fix: Guard on non-empty text before charging: after line 145 add `if (!text) return NextResponse.json({ text: "" });` (return without consuming), or wrap 148-149 in `if (text && device && !unlimited)` so an empty/all-non-text 200 never burns a credit.

title: connectionsDurable test only passes because in-memory upsertConnection is synchronous; the real fire-and-forget durable write is unobservable to it
reasoning: The core mechanism is accurate and grounded. addConnection/setConnectionStatus/removeConnection persist via `void getAccounts().upsertConnection(c).catch(()=>{})` (memoryStore.ts:206,222,226) — fire-and-forget, errors swallowed. In the test path getAccounts() returns MemoryAccountsStore, whose upsertConnection (store.ts:237) is `async` but writes synchronously — `this.m.conns.set(c.id, {...c})` runs with NO preceding await, so the map write completes before addConnection returns, i.e. before resetStoreForTests() at connectionsDurable.test.ts:33. So the test only passes because the in-memory write is synchronous. The real SupabaseAccountsStore.upsertConnection (store.ts:437-443) is an awaited network round-trip fired without await; a redeploy before the write flushes would silently lose the connection, and the test cannot observe that race, a rejected promise, or a partial write. I confirmed no test sets SUPABASE_URL/SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (grep found none) and there is no flush/await of pending writes, so the Supabase durability path is exercised by zero tests. The test proves memory-fallback bookkeeping, not real redeploy durability — this part of the claim is confirmed. HOWEVER one sub-point is factually wrong: the claim cites a schema mismatch from the extra display_name/backfill_progress/created_at/updated_at columns, but db/migrations/0002_durable.sql:36-45 declares all four columns, so the real upsert would NOT hit a schema mismatch. schema.test.ts:76 does under-specify osmo_connections to only id/device_id/platform/status, so those columns are untested at the contract level, but there is no actual mismatch to fail on. Because this is a test-coverage/observability gap (the durability guarantee is asserted only against the sync memory stub) rather than a live production defect, and the schema-mismatch example is refuted, I revise severity from medium to low.
fix: Add a Supabase-path integration test (or a fake AccountsStore whose upsertConnection resolves asynchronously) so the fire-and-forget durable write is actually awaited/observed; and expand schema.test.ts DURABLE_CONTRACT for osmo_connections to include display_name, backfill_progress, created_at, updated_at. Separately, consider awaiting the durable write (or a flush hook) instead of pure fire-and-forget to close the real redeploy-race window.

title: observability test claims to cover the db readiness probe but the probe branch never runs in tests
evidence: web/tests/observability.test.ts:31-34 (comment: 'n/a when no Supabase in tests'); web/app/api/health/route.ts (if accountsAreLive() { ... } branch)
reasoning: Verified against the cited code. In web/app/api/health/route.ts (lines 16-20), `db` is initialized to 'n/a' and only reassigned to 'ok'/'down' inside `if (accountsAreLive()) { try { await getAccounts().deviceById('__health_probe__'); db='ok'; } catch { db='down'; } }`. In web/lib/accounts/store.ts (lines 477-479), `accountsAreLive()` returns true only when both SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY are set. I confirmed neither the global setup (web/tests/setup.ts) nor web/tests/observability.test.ts sets those vars, and vitest.config.ts uses only that setup file — a repo-wide grep found no SUPABASE_URL/SERVICE_ROLE_KEY anywhere under tests/. So during tests `accountsAreLive()` is always false, the probe branch never runs, and `db` is always the literal 'n/a'. The assertion at line 32, `expect(body.ready).toHaveProperty('db')`, only checks the property exists — it passes on 'n/a' and would still pass even if the ok/down probe logic were broken, so the readiness probe's actual point (surfacing a DB-down condition) is genuinely unexercised. The comment on line 32 ('n/a when no Supabase in tests') even acknowledges this. This is a real test-coverage gap. Severity stays low: the production probe code itself reads correct; there is no runtime defect or user impact, only an untested branch.
fix: Add a test that sets SUPABASE_URL/SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (and stubs getAccounts().deviceById to resolve then throw) so accountsAreLive() is true, asserting body.ready.db === 'ok' in the happy case and === 'down' with body.ok === false when the probe throws.

title: db migration runner (db/apply.mjs) has zero test coverage despite being the mechanism that provisions the durable schema
evidence: web/db/apply.mjs (new, 88 lines); web/tests/schema.test.ts:37-45 (parses files only); no tests/ reference to apply.mjs
reasoning: The literal claim is true: grep of tests/ for apply.mjs/db/apply/db:migrate/osmo_migrations returns nothing, and schema.test.ts only statically parses .sql text (it reads files and asserts declared columns, lines 26-69). No test executes the runner. But the three risks the claim leans on to justify importance are refuted by the code. (1) "statement-splitting": apply.mjs has NO splitter — the only split() is env-file line parsing (line 24); it hands the whole file to pg via a single client.query(sql) (line 77), so there is no custom splitting logic to test. (2) "can inadvertently touch non-osmo objects": every DDL object in both migrations is osmo_* (25 create-table-if-not-exists, all indexes/RLS scoped to osmo_*); the one dynamic loop, 0002 lines 191-198, iterates a hardcoded literal array of osmo_* names. apply.mjs itself contains zero table-name logic — it only executes literal file text — so there is no runner code path that could reach a non-osmo object; this is structurally impossible, not merely unverified. (3) idempotency IS a real testable property (osmo_migrations ledger + done.has(file) skip at line 72, if-not-exists throughout, per-migration begin/commit/rollback at 75-83) and it is untested by execution — but the module is a ~30-line human-gated operational script: it is a clean no-op when DATABASE_URL is unset (lines 41-49), pg isn't even installed, no CI/.github invokes db:migrate, and the header documents applying-for-real as an explicit human gate. The claim's assertion that the green schema test "gives the impression migrations are validated end-to-end" is contradicted by that test's own header, which states "No database required: it parses ... statically." So: real-but-trivial coverage gap, correctly low severity, but the shared-DB/non-osmo/statement-splitting framing is unsupported. Hence REVISED rather than confirmed.
fix: Add a lightweight unit test that imports the runner's migrationFiles()/split logic OR runs apply.mjs twice against a throwaway Postgres (e.g. pg-mem or a CI service container) asserting the second run reports "skip ... (already applied)" — i.e. cover idempotency of the osmo_migrations ledger; the non-osmo-touch and statement-splitting concerns need no test because no such code path exists.
